submission.raw_text:
My name is Alex Rivera and I’m a freelance web designer. In early April I agreed to build a five-page marketing website for Maya Chen’s consulting business for $2,400. She paid a $1,200 deposit, with the balance due when the project was completed. The signed agreement is attached.

The intended launch was around May 20, although the contract also says the timeline depended on Maya supplying final copy and images by April 25. She did not send everything by then. I think the last major batch arrived around May 8 or May 9, but some small text changes kept coming after that.

The original job was a homepage, about page, services page, contact page, and mobile-responsive layout, with two revision rounds. During the project Maya also asked for a pricing comparison section, a newsletter signup, and several changes to the homepage design. I accepted most of those requests through WhatsApp because I wanted to keep the client happy. I probably should have documented more clearly which requests were outside the original scope.

I sent what I considered a complete staging version on June 3. Maya replied that it “looked really good overall” but gave me a list of changes. I made most of them during the following week. She later said the project was too late, that the mobile version had major problems, and that the delay had damaged a marketing campaign she planned to run.

There were some mobile issues, but I do not agree that the website was unusable. I fixed the issues I could reproduce. Maya also used images from the website in social media posts, and I believe at least part of the site was briefly published, although I cannot prove exactly what was live or for how long.

Maya now refuses to pay the remaining $1,200 and has also said she may ask for the deposit back. She says I disappeared for two weeks. That is exaggerated. I was slower for part of one week because of a family issue, but I still replied to messages and told her what was happening.

I feel like she is using the missed target date as an excuse not to pay for work she received. I am asking for the remaining $1,200. I will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials when the balance is paid. I want JuryAI to consider that much of the schedule delay came from late content, added requests, and repeated changes.

open_issues: The agreement itself has not been inspected.
The calendar year for the agreement, target dates, content deadline, and June 3 delivery is not supplied.
The narrative calls the project a five-page website but names four pages plus a mobile-responsive layout.
It is unclear whether transfer of editable source files and administrator credentials upon payment was an express contract term or Alex's present position.
The meaning of project completion and any acceptance procedure have not been established from the uninspected agreement.

evidence.title: Signed project agreement

limitations: The agreement was mentioned as attached but was not inspected during this narrative extraction.
Its exact wording, signatures, completeness, and authenticity have not been verified.

limitations: No messages or export were inspected.
The communications may not clearly identify which requests were outside the original scope.

limitations: The staging version was not inspected.
Its completeness, functionality, mobile behavior, and June 3 state cannot be assessed.

claim_evidence_links.explanation: Alex identifies the uninspected signed agreement as the source of the original scope.
The agreement allegedly states the total price, deposit, balance, and payment trigger.
The agreement allegedly identifies an intended launch around May 20.
The agreement allegedly conditions the timeline on delivery of copy and images by April 25.
Alex says the added requests were communicated and accepted through WhatsApp.
The uninspected WhatsApp history may show whether added requests were identified as out-of-scope.
The staging version is the deliverable Alex characterizes as complete.
The described message contains the positive phrase attributed to Maya.
The change list may qualify the extent of any positive feedback or acceptance.
The alleged posts may show use of images derived from the website.
Alex identifies alleged brief publication as additional use but says he cannot prove its scope or duration.
The alleged client-content dependency bears on responsibility for delay.
The message history may establish the timing and volume of added requests and repeated changes.

damages_claims.causal_theory: Alex claims the remaining contractual balance became due when he delivered a complete staging version and performed most subsequent requested changes, but Maya refuses to pay.
damages_claims.calculation_basis: $2,400 stated contract price minus the $1,200 deposit already paid.

required_actions: After payment of the remaining balance, Alex will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials to Maya.
outcomes.rationale: Alex seeks the unpaid contractual balance for work he says was completed and delivered, while offering transfer of final files and credentials upon payment.

clarification_questions.question: What calendar year applies to the early-April agreement, April 25 content deadline, May 8 or May 9 content delivery, May 20 launch target, and June 3 staging delivery?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative provides month and day references without a year, so the project timeline cannot be dated reliably.

clarification_questions.question: What was the fifth page in the agreed five-page website, and was the mobile-responsive layout a separate deliverable rather than that fifth page?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative names only four pages while describing the project as a five-page website.

clarification_questions.question: Which pricing-section, newsletter-signup, and homepage-change requests did you accept, which did you complete, and did any WhatsApp message identify them as outside scope or change the price or deadline?
clarification_questions.reason: The scope, completion status, and schedule effect of the added requests are material but currently unclear.

clarification_questions.question: What items on Maya's post-staging change list remained unfinished, and what specific mobile issues existed, were reproduced, and were fixed?
clarification_questions.reason: These details are needed to assess Alex's completion claim and the materiality of the acknowledged mobile issues.

clarification_questions.question: What copy and images were missing on April 25, and what messages, timestamps, or delivery records show when the major batch and later text changes arrived?
clarification_questions.reason: Late content is central to Alex's explanation for delay, but no supporting content-delivery evidence is presently described.

clarification_questions.question: Does the signed agreement expressly state that editable source files and administrator credentials are transferred only after final payment? If so, what does that provision say?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative does not establish whether the proposed payment-before-transfer sequence is contractual or merely Alex's current position.
